coredata.dc:title: Non-thermal effect of microwave on the chemical structure and luminescence properties of biomass-derived carbon dots via hydrothermal method
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yinghui
Guo, Dandan
Gao, Ying
Tong, Bihai
Li, Yu
Zhu, Yuezhao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) is a burgeoning nanomaterial; in particular, those derived from biomass are more sustainable, economical and eco-friendly. The microwave-assisted hydrothermal (MHTC) method was increasingly used to prepare biomass-based CDs as a new green technology. However, the non-thermal effect of microwave remains controversial. In this work, the microwave non-thermal effect in MHTC preparation of CDs from soymeal (S-CDs) was confirmed through comparative experiments between microwave-assisted and conventional hydrothermal (HTC) method. S-CDs prepared by the MHTC method obtained higher relative proportions of self-doping nitrogen, pyridine N, amine N, quaternary N and conjugate sp2 C than that by the HTC method for the microwave non-thermal effect, which led to stronger luminescence and red shifts of the absorption and emission. The optimization effect of microwave non-thermal action on S-CDs structure and luminescence performance would not disappear with reaction temperature and residence time. The luminescence intensities of S-CDs obtained by the MHTC method were all stronger than that by the HTC method among the different reaction conditions. The performance optimization caused by microwave non-thermal effect could be briefly explained as macroscopic luminescence enhancement due to more fluorescent carbon nano-particles and microscopic luminescence enhancement due to the formation of key luminescence chemical structures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave
Non-thermal effect
Biomass-derived carbon dots
Hydrothermal method
Physicochemical property
Optical property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent S-CDs by MHTC and HTC methods
Microwave on physicochemical properties of S-CDs
Microwave on optical properties of S-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process of the B-CDs by MHTC and HTC methods.
FTIR spectra of the S-CDs synthesized via MHTC and HTC methods.

(a) SMHTC-CDs-, (b) SHTC-CDs -XPS full-scan survey spectra; (c) SMHTC-CDs -, (d) SHTC-CDs -high-resolution XPS of C1s; (e) SMHTC-, (f) SHTC-CDs-high-resolution XPS of N1s; (g) SMHTC-, (h) SHTC-high-resolution XPS of O1s.

Images of S-CDs synthesized via MHTC and HTC in (a) supernatants, (b) supernatants under UV light, (c) supernatants diluted to fivefold volume, (d) supernatants diluted to fivefold volume under UV light, (e) supernatants diluted to colorless, (f) supernatants diluted to colorless under UV light.
UV–Vis spectra of S-CDs synthesized via MHTC and HTC.
PL emission spectra of S-CDs synthesized via (a) MHTC-200 ℃, 1 h, (b) HTC-200 ℃, 1 h, (c) MHTC-180 ℃, 2 h, (d) HTC-180 ℃, 2 h.
PL emission spectra of S-CDs synthesized by MHTC and HTC at different reaction temperatures.
PL emission spectra of S-CDs synthesized by MHTC and HTC at different residence times.
(a) SMHTC-CDs and (b) SHTC-CDs were used as fluorescent ink.
Percentage of C, N and their chemical states in the S-CDs.

head.ce:title: Non-thermal effect of microwave on the chemical structure and luminescence properties of biomass-derived carbon dots via hydrothermal method

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil Engineering and Architechitecture, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan 243032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Civil Engineering and Architechitecture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Civil Engineering and Architechitecture, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan 243032, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Power Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Power Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The College of Energy, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Metallurgy Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan 243032, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Metallurgy Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan 243032, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) is a burgeoning nanomaterial; in particular, those derived from biomass are more sustainable, economical and eco-friendly. The microwave-assisted hydrothermal (MHTC) method was increasingly used to prepare biomass-based CDs as a new green technology. However, the non-thermal effect of microwave remains controversial. In this work, the microwave non-thermal effect in MHTC preparation of CDs from soymeal (S-CDs) was confirmed through comparative experiments between microwave-assisted and conventional hydrothermal (HTC) method. S-CDs prepared by the MHTC method obtained higher relative proportions of self-doping nitrogen, pyridine N, amine N, quaternary N and conjugate sp2 C than that by the HTC method for the microwave non-thermal effect, which led to stronger luminescence and red shifts of the absorption and emission. The optimization effect of microwave non-thermal action on S-CDs structure and luminescence performance would not disappear with reaction temperature and residence time. The luminescence intensities of S-CDs obtained by the MHTC method were all stronger than that by the HTC method among the different reaction conditions. The performance optimization caused by microwave non-thermal effect could be briefly explained as macroscopic luminescence enhancement due to more fluorescent carbon nano-particles and microscopic luminescence enhancement due to the formation of key luminescence chemical structures.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) is a novel kind of carbon nanomaterial with excellent properties of multicolor fluorescence, robust chemical inertness, aqueous solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility, etc., which applies in many fields [1,2]. Since it was first discovered in 2004 [3], it had been widely studied by researchers all over the world and prepared by various methods. Among these methods, the hydrothermal (HTC) method is being extensively studied because it is not restricted by the moisture content of raw materials, convenient and environmentally friendly. The advantage of independent of raw materials water content is especially suitable for biomass with different moisture levels. Therefore, hydrothermal disposal of biomass for available products has become an important and dominating conversion technology in the face of today's energy and resource crisis and environmental problems. Many scholars converted biomass into bio-oil fuel, biochar or various functional carbon materials by the HTC method [4–6]. The conversion of biomass into biomass-based CDs by the HTC method is one of the important ways for the high-value utilization of biomass. However, after extensive research, the HTC method had exposed the problems of long residence time, low yield of nanoparticles and high energy consumption [7]. Thereupon, the microwave field was added to the HTC method and gradually employed as a new green technology by many scholars for its many advantages such as rapid reaction, high product yield, good product quality, high reproducibility, energy conservation and eco-friendly [8–11].

Microwave is the electromagnetic wave with frequency ranging from 300 MHz–300 GHz and wavelength at 1 m–1 mm. Raw materials are heated in the microwave field through the interaction of electromagnetic waves with the electric dipole of molecular [7], which is a molecular level heating method [12]. Microwave heating is very different from conventional heating. Conventional heating is to conduct heat from the outer boundary of raw materials to the inner through heat conduction, convection and radiation, while microwave interacts with raw material molecular, generating heat through the entire bulk of the materials rapidly and uniformly. Consequently, the in situ and transient microwave heating method can remarkably improve the product yields, the final product qualities and obtain new materials that could not be synthesized by conventional methods.

At present, more and more researchers adopted microwave hydrothermal (MHTC) technology to synthesize CDs. Rodríguez-Padrón et al. prepared fluorescent CDs with an average diameter of 17.5 nm and tunable emissions dependent on excitation wavelengths from lignocellulosic residues through the MHTC method [13]. Eskalen et al. used cotton linter as a carbon source to synthesize CDs by the MHTC method and characterize the morphological and optical properties of the CDs, and further experimentally studied its performance in fluorescence cancer imaging and human cell growth inhibition [14]. Guo et al. adopted MHTC method to prepare CDs with green emission, good water dispersity and cell imaging properties by using Glu and amino group of MPD as raw materials [10]. It was clear that most of the relevant studies directly used the MHTC method to prepare CDs, describing the properties of products and trying application experiments. There were few reports on whether microwave effect existed in the process of preparing CDs or what specific effects microwave had on the structure, physicochemical and photoluminescence (PL) characteristics of CDs. Omer and Sartin obtained N-doped CDs that could be used as a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode probe by the HTC method followed by microwave irradiation. They found that the relative fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs after the microwave irradiation was 5% higher than that before. Thus, they believed that microwave had an enhanced effect on fluorescence [15]. However, it is a pity that they could not go deep into the reasons for this enhancement or the effect of microwave on the structure and physiochemical properties of CDs.

The microwave effects, especially the non-thermal (athermal) effect of microwave, were still in question [9]. For the moment, some articles reported the thermal effect of microwave, which could significantly shorten the reaction time [16,17]; however, the researches on the non-thermal effect of microwave produced two completely different conclusions. Singh et al. investigated and reported the existence of a non-thermal effect in microwave-assisted synthesis of quantum dots [12]. Liu et al. confirmed and discussed the non-thermal effect of microwave in the alkali hydrolysis of wheat straw [18]. On the contrary, both Zhou et al. and Shazman et al. failed to detect any significant non-thermal effects in the composition or characteristics of products or Maillard reaction [19,20]. Then, is there a microwave non-thermal effect in the hydrothermal preparation of CDs? Does the microwave affect the structure and properties of CDs in addition to the rapid rise of temperature? In particular, the biomass composition is complex, so exploring microwave effects on CDs derived from biomass (B-CDs) is of great significance for regulating the performance of B-CDs through adjusting the microwave field. It is necessary to carry out comparative experiments between microwave and conventional heating for clarifying microwave effects.

In this work, we attempted to explore whether microwave non-thermal effect existed in MHTC preparation of B-CDs from biomass and whether this non-thermal effect impacted on the structure and luminescence characteristics of the B-CDs. The representative biomass soymeal in our previous report [21] was still selected as raw material. The microwave effect was studied through the comparative hydrothermal experiments with and without microwave-assisted under the same reaction conditions. Various characterization methods were used to analyze and differentiate the structure and luminescence characteristics of the B-CDs by the two preparation methods. Thus, the differences in the structure and properties of B-CDs caused by microwave non-thermal effect were revealed. The correlation between reaction conditions and microwave non-thermal effect was studied to investigate the changes in the luminescence performance caused by the non-thermal effect of the microwave when reaction temperature and residence time changed.

Soymeal was purchased from the countryside of Jiangsu, China. It was first dried at 105 °C for 8 h and then crushed and sifted by a 60 mesh sifter to minimize the effect of particle size on S-CDs formation. Before the reaction, the soymeal powder was mixed well with water so that the raw material was uniformly heated during the HTC process as far as possible, and the interaction with microwave was enhanced during the MHTC process. Deionized (DI) water was supplied by the Smart-Q DI water purification system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent S-CDs by MHTC and HTC methods
ce:section.ce:para: S-CDs were prepared by MHTC and HTC methods (abbreviated SMHTC-CDs and SHTC-CDs, respectively) under the working conditions of 180℃ for 1, 2, 3 h and 200 ℃ for 1 h. The method of preparing CDs by MHTC method was the same as our previous report [21]. Briefly, the raw material was thoroughly mixed with water in a ratio of 1:25 and transferred to a Teflon™-lined reaction vessel. The vessel was sealed and put into a microwave workstation (XO-SM100; Nanjing Xianou, China). Next, the material was heated to 200 ℃ with magnetic stirring by microwave heating and maintained for 1 h (taking 200 ℃, 1 h as an example). After the reaction, the vessel was cooled to room temperature. The products were ultracentrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min to separate the supernatant from the large carbon particles. For better separation, the centrifugation process was repeated twice. Then, the supernatant containing SMHTC-CDs was extracted and dialyzed against DI water by a dialysis membrane with the molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da for 1 ~ 2 days. Part of the supernatant was stored in the original solution state, and part was freeze-dried for 60 h by a lyophilizer for the subsequent characterization.

In order to analyze the microwave effect, preparing SHTC-CDs by HTC method under the same reaction conditions was served as a comparative experiment. The raw material and water were fully mixed in the same proportion of 1:25, poured into the reaction vessel and sealed in the hydrothermal autoclave. After that, they were heated to the same reaction temperature of 200 ℃ for the same residence time of 1 h by the traditional heating method. Then the vessel was cooled to room temperature. The products were initially separated by suction filtration, and then the supernatant was treated in the same steps as above, such as centrifugation, dialysis and freeze-drying. The obtained samples were then characterized. In order to minimize the occasional error of the experiments and ensure that there were sufficient samples for subsequent tests, the experiments were repeated at least five times for each working condition. Then, the samples obtained from repeated experiments were processed together for follow-up tests to ensure that the experimental data were not only representative of a certain experimental result but more general and scientific. The B-CDs preparation process by MHTC and HTC methods was briefly illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The morphology of the S-CDs was determined by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM-2100F). The crystalline structure of the S-CDs was analyzed through X-ray powder diffraction patterns (XRD, Miniflex 600) within the 2θ range of 5° to 70° with Cu radiation (λ = 0.154 nm). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) experiments on a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer with KBr pellets were carried out to investigate the surface functionalization of the S-CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to determine the chemical structure and element states of the S-CDs on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI electron spectrometer by Al Kα X-ray radiation (hv = 1486.6 eV) as the excitation source. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yields (PL QYs) were measured by an FLS980 steady/transient state fluorescence spectrometer with a photomultiplier tube sphere as the Eml detector’s light source and a xenon XEL lamp as the light source. UV–Vis absorption spectra were acquired by using a TU-1901 spectrometer at room temperature. Fluorescence measurements of the S-CDs were performed at various excitation wavelengths on a PerkinElmer LS55 spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave on physicochemical properties of S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It took about 16 min to raise the reaction temperature to 200 ℃ with microwave-assisted, while it took about 50 min ~ 1 h without microwave in the hydrothermal process. It could be seen that the microwave had an obvious thermal effect, which made the reactants heat up faster. The physicochemical and luminescence characteristics of S-CDs prepared by the two methods were analyzed below to investigate whether microwave had non-thermal action on CDs synthesis reaction and what specific effect it had.

In our previous report, the S-CDs was determined to be a spherical morphology with lattice spacings and an amorphous structure with poor crystallinity based on the TEM and XRD results [21]. Due to the contingency of sample preparation and observed sample area during the TEM test, the distribution of CDs on the sample stage was accidental and unrepresentative, so the S-CDs prepared by the two methods were not directly compared through TEM results.

FTIR spectra of S-CDs were prepared by MHTC and HTC methods at 200 ℃ for 1 h, as shown in Fig. 2 . The FTIR peaks of the SMHTC-CDs were more obvious than SHTC-CDs. A broad band around 3400 cm−1, corresponding to − OH stretching vibration, appeared both in SMHTC-CDs and SHTC-CDs [22]. It's worth noting that the stretching vibration of − NH at 3251 cm−1 only appeared in the SMHTC-CDs [23]. Within the range of 1080 ~ 1294 cm−1, the spectrum of SMHTC-CDs had four characteristic peaks attributed to C − O and C − N stretching vibrations that were almost absent from the spectrum of SHTC-CDs. In addition, the out-of-plane bending vibration of C − H bonds with aromatic structure around the range of 611 ~ 671 cm−1 could also be seen only in the SMHTC-CDs spectrum. In the range of 1595 ~ 1670 cm−1, the sharp peaks of SMHTC-CDs corresponding to C = C and − COOH vibration absorptions were more significant than that of SHTC-CDs, especially the C = C structure of SHTC-CDs related to intrinsic luminescence was very weak [24,25]. The peak at 1402 cm−1 corresponded to the deformation vibration of C − H. The small shoulder band at 1770 cm−1 due to the C = O functional group could not be ignored even if it was not obvious for being overlapped by C = C and − COOH vibration, which appeared in the SMHTC-CDs spectrum [26]. The characteristic bands at 1454 cm−1 were attributed to C − N stretching vibration of amide III, and this peak in the SMHTC-CDs spectrum was also more obvious than that in the SHTC-CDs spectrum. It was thus evident that the key characteristic peaks related to luminescence such as C = C, C = O, −NH, C − N and C − O in SMHTC-CDs spectrum were more significant, which were very weak or almost disappeared in SHTC-CDs spectrum [21].

The above analysis indicated that the SMHTC-CDs prepared by the MHTC method obtained more oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups, completed the self-doping of N better, and had a better sp2 C(C = C) skeleton structure than the SHTC-CDs. Under the same reaction temperature and residence time, the difference in functional properties of S-CDs caused by microwave indicated that the effect of microwave on the chemical reaction was not only due to thermal effect but also closely related to the non-thermal effect of microwave. Based on the microscopic interpretation, the non-thermal action of microwave also affected the chemical bonds through microwave optical photon energy. Microwave did not directly cause the change of chemical bonds, because its optical photon energy was relatively lower than the energy required for breaking a chemical bond. Instead, some chemical bonds were broken when their bond energy was weakened in the process of chemical reaction to form new chemical bonds under microwave action [27]. The microwave could change the activation energy and reaction kinetics, and it was selective to the heating of reactants [28]. Continuously, the non-thermal effect of microwave promoted the reaction of cracking, cross-linking, agglomeration, condensation and carbonization and thus, more carbon nano-particles were generated. The carbon particles absorbed microwave energy as good microwave-absorbers for their carbonaceous nature, and then the energy increased the temperature inside and the surrounding of the reactor until the above reaction conditions were met, thereby facilitating the reaction and possibly generating more CDs [29]. On the other hand, the non-thermal effect of microwave on chemical reactions made the CDs complete nitrogen self-doping to a higher degree and have better nitrogen- and oxygen- functional characteristics and sp2 C structure, which would be conducive to the surface state and intrinsic state luminescence of CDs.

Fig. 3 showed the XPS spectra of SMHTC-CDs and SHTC-CDs, respectively. The chemical structure and element chemical states of S-CDs by the two methods were compared, and the non-thermal action of microwave on the formation of element chemical states was analyzed. According to the XPS full-scan spectra (Fig. 3(a), (b)), the S-CDs synthesized by the two methods both contained carbon (C1s, 284.9 eV), nitrogen (N1s, 398.9 eV) and oxygen (O1s, 530.9 eV), but the relative contents of which were different, SMHTC-CDs: C, 65.65%, N, 12.55%, O, 21.80%; SHTC-CDs: C, 70.69%, N, 10.20%, O, 19.11%, respectively. Obviously, SMHTC-CDs had higher nitrogen content. Under the same condition of the raw material, the ratio of raw materials to water, residence time and reaction temperature, the microwave effect made more self-doping nitrogen in the process of reaction to generate S-CDs. Moreover, when the reaction temperature set in this paper was 200 ℃, even if the residence time was only 1 h, the N-doping ratio of S-CDs was not only higher than that (7.2%) of our previous work reported (180℃, 2 h), but also higher than that of Sheng, et al. [30] (10.1%) and Liang, et al. [23] (8.88%) reported to add ethylenediamine as the nitrogen source, which suggested that the increase of reaction temperature promoted the increase of nitrogen-doping degree and the content of self-doped nitrogen in this paper was considerable without adding any adscititious nitrogen source. The C1s spectra of S-CDs (Fig. 3(c), (d)) presented five main peaks at 284.5 (sp2 C [C = C]), 284.8 (sp3 C [C − C]), 286 (C − N), 287.3 (C − O) and 288.1 (C = O) eV [13,26]. The N1s spectra (Fig. 3(e), (f)) exhibited four peaks at 398.5, 399.7, 400.2 and 401.3 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, amine N, pyrrolic N and quaternary N [31]. The O1s spectra (Fig. 3(g), (h)) were fitted by two peaks, which were attributed to C = O (531.3 eV) and C − OH/C − O − C. It could be seen that the relative contents of different chemical states of each element of SMHTC-CDs and SHTC-CDs were significantly different, as listed in Table 1 .

As shown in Table 1, the relative proportions of pyridine N, amine N, quaternary N, C − N, C − O and C = O in SMHTC-CDs were all higher than that in SHTC-CDs, and these structures were closely related to surface state luminescence. The conjugate structure sp2 C could dominate intrinsic state luminescence [32], and a higher ratio of sp2 C to sp3 C content was also obtained by SMHTC-CDs. According to the analysis results in our previous article, pyridinic N, amine N and sp2 C could reduce band gap, and quaternary N contributed to the anti-interference of CDs [21]. All of the above indicated that the non-thermal effect of microwave played a crucial role in the formation of the luminescent chemical structure of CDs. It was proved again that microwave optical photon energy did have a significant effect on chemical bonds, which promoted the formation of some key chemical bonds that could optimize the luminescence performance of CDs under the conditions discussed in this paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave on optical properties of S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The supernatants of SMHTC -CDs and SHTC -CDs were shown in Fig. 4 (a). Fig. 4 (b) showed the luminescence of the supernatant solutions under UV light. The color of the original supernatant solutions prepared by the two methods was approximately the same, but the PL of SMHTC -CDs was stronger than that of SHTC -CDs. The original supernatants presented a certain inner filter effect for the high-concentration S-CDs. The inner filter effect made the luminescence relatively dim and opaque. When the supernatants were diluted to five-sold volumes (Fig. 4 [c] and [d]), the inner filter effect was weakened and the fluorescence became brighter and more transparent than before. The luminescence of SMHTC -CDs was still stronger than that of SHTC -CDs. When the supernatants were further diluted to colorless, they still emitted bright cyan PL and the PL corresponding to SMHTC -CDs was still stronger. The inner filter effect disappeared, and the fluorescence was clear and transparent. The absolute PL QYs of the SMHTC -CDs and SHTC -CDs measured were 4.92% and 4.62%, respectively, which was consistent with the previous analysis and proved that the introduction of the microwave field enhanced the luminescence.

The non-thermal effect of microwave caused differences in structure, which led to differences in luminescence characteristics. The differences in optical properties of the SMHTC-CDs and SHTC-CDs were further analyzed through UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectroscopy. Fig. 5 showed the normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra. The absorption characteristics of S-CDs obtained by the two methods were similar to the Gaussian peak, but the peaks were located at 316 (SHTC-CDs) and 327 nm (SMHTC-CDs), respectively. The Gaussian characteristic could be due to the surface state, such as oxygen-, nitrogen-containing functional groups or fluorophores [32]. According to the peak position, the absorption of the two methods was attributed to the n–π* transition, corresponding to the effect of nonbonding orbitals from pyridinic N [31]. The π–π* transition derived from the conjugated C = C of carbon core, locating the peaks at < 300 nm. Therefore, it could be concluded that the absorption characteristic of S-CDs was dominated by the surface state. Because of the microwave non-thermal effect, the absorption peak shifted from 316 to 327 nm. The absorption redshift was due to the bandgap reduction. As the above structural analysis, the relative ratios of Pyridinic N, amine N, quaternary N, C–N, C = O and sp2 C/sp3 C in SMHTC-CDs were all higher than those in SHTC-CDs, which indicated that the non-thermal action of microwave made the surface states and carbon core states related to luminescence more abundant so that the band gap was reduced and the red shift occurred.

The fluorescence emission spectra were measured using excitation wavelengths from 280 to 500 nm at intervals of 20 nm, as shown in Fig. 6 . The SMHTC-CDs and SHTC-CDs obtained at 200 ℃, 1 h and 180 ℃, 2 h were tested, respectively. Under the condition of 200 ℃, 1 h, the excitation wavelengths of the maximum PL intensity for the S-CDs prepared by the two methods both were 400 nm, as shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b). However, the optimal emission peak of the maximum PL intensity for SMHTC-CDs was located at 485 nm, slightly redshift compared with the optimal emission peak at 482 nm for SHTC-CDs. When the reaction condition was 180 ℃, 2 h, the optimal excitation wavelengths of S-CDs obtained by the two methods were 420 (SMHTC-CDs, Fig. 6[c])) and 400 nm (SHTC-CDs, Fig. 6[d])) respectively, which suggested that microwave non-thermal effect reduced the excitation energy required for SMHTC-CDs. Moreover, there was a large redshift of optimal emission peak position from 484 nm (SHTC-CDs) to 499 nm (SMHTC-CDs), which indicated that the microwave non-thermal effect reduced the band gap to make the luminescence of SMHTC-CDs move to longer emission wavelengths. Obtaining CDs with long-wavelength emission characteristics has always been one of the difficulties in the research fields [33]. The potential effect of microwave non-thermal action that enabled CDs luminescence to move toward the longer wavelength could expand the applications of CDs in the fields such as bioimaging and medical diagnosis.

Finally, the fluorescence stability of the S-CDs was tested. The supernatants of SMHTC-CDs and SHTC-CDs were used as fluorescent ink to draw the patterns on paper without background fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 9 . It could be seen that the painted patterns had bright color rendering fluorescence. Moreover, no evident fluorescence quenching occurred after a long time of strong UV-light irradiation, which suggested the prepared S-CDs had good stability against photobleaching. There was no obvious difference observed in the fluorescence stability of SMHTC-CDs and SHTC-CDs.

In this work, the non-thermal effect of microwave on the physiochemical structure and properties of S-CDs was confirmed and researched through the comparative hydrothermal experiments with and without microwave-assisted under the same reaction conditions. SMHTC-CDs obtained more oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups and higher self-doping nitrogen content than SHTC-CDs. The key chemical structures of SMHTC-CDs, such as pyridine N, amine N, quaternary N and conjugate sp2 C, also gained higher relative proportions for the non-thermal effect of microwave, which led to stronger luminescence and red shifts of the absorption and emission. The optimization effect of microwave non-thermal action on S-CDs structure and luminescence performance would not disappear with the change of reaction temperature and residence time. The PL intensities of SMHTC-CDs were all stronger than that of SHTC-CDs among the several different reaction conditions. The performance optimization caused by microwave non-thermal effect could be briefly explained from two aspects: first, the non-thermal action promoted the reaction of cracking, cross-linking and polymerization to generate more carbon nano-particles, thus enhancing the macroscopic luminescence; second, microwave optical photon energy promoted the formation of key chemical structures that reduced the band gap, thus enhancing the microscopic luminescence. This work provided a new way for the regulation of CDs performance. Next, we will attempt to regulate the structure and performance of CDs by changing microwave field conditions.

Yinghui Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Dandan Guo: . Ying Gao: Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Bihai Tong: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yu Li: Project administration. Yuezhao Zhu: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Scientific Research Foundation of Anhui University of Technology, the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20201365), the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (51406063 and 51676096), Aero-engine Thermal Environment and Structure Key Laboratory of Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (CEPE2019008), Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Process Enhancement and New Energy Equipment Technology and the Top-notch Academic Program Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Algae biomass as a precursor for synthesis of nitrogen-and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots: A better probe in Arabidopsis guard cells and root tissues
J. Photochem. Photobiol., B
